title: Tarhonya
subtitle: Hungarian egg-barley pasta with paprika and onion
excerpt: Tiny pearl-shaped pasta toasted in fat with paprika and onion until golden, then simmered in stock until each grain is tender and separate. The classic Hungarian side dish, served with paprikash, goulash, or braised meats.
sourceNotes: Based on the Hungarian tarhonya tradition as documented in George Lang, 'The Cuisine of Hungary' (1971). Tarhonya is a sun-dried egg pasta unique to Hungarian and some Balkan cuisines; the commercial variety is available dried, and the toasting step is what gives it its characteristic nuttiness.

content.text: Tarhonya is a dried pasta unique to the Hungarian kitchen. The grains are roughly the size and shape of large barley kernels, formed from egg and flour, rolled into pellets, and sun-dried. The cooking method is closer to pilaf than to boiling pasta: the dried grains are first toasted in lard or butter with onion and paprika, then stock is added and the whole thing simmers covered until the liquid is absorbed and the grains are tender.

The toasting step matters. Three to four minutes in fat, stirring constantly, takes the pasta from pale beige to deep gold and gives the finished dish a nutty depth that plain boiled tarhonya lacks. Don't rush this stage.
Dried tarhonya is sold in Hungarian food shops and online. If unavailable, giant couscous or orzo pasta cooked the same way produces a similar result in texture if not entirely in flavour.

items.name: Giant couscous
items.prepNote: tarhonya if available; giant couscous is a reasonable substitute

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: or lard for a more traditional result

content.text: Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the saucepan over a medium heat. Add {{onion}} finely chopped onion and cook for 5 minutes until soft. Add {{giant-couscous}} of tarhonya (or giant couscous) and stir constantly for 3 to 4 minutes until the grains are golden and smell nutty. Add {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika and stir for 30 seconds.

Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of warm chicken stock and season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Stir once, cover tightly, and reduce the heat to low. Cook for 12 to 15 minutes without lifting the lid. Check: the stock should be absorbed and the grains tender but separate.
Remove from the heat and leave covered for 3 minutes. Uncover and fluff with a fork. Serve as a side to chicken paprikash, veal paprikash, or any braised meat.

items.fix: Toast the grains properly before adding liquid. Resist lifting the lid during the simmer. Fluff gently with a fork, not a spoon, after resting.
items.cause: Lid lifted during cooking, releasing steam; or stock added before grains were properly toasted
items.symptom: Grains are stuck together in clumps

items.fix: Add 50 ml more stock, re-cover, and cook for a further 5 minutes on low.
items.cause: Heat too high so stock boiled away before grains cooked through
items.symptom: Stock absorbed but grains are still hard

items.fix: Uncover and cook over medium heat for 2 to 3 minutes to drive off excess moisture.
items.cause: Too much stock or cooked for too long

items.fix: There is no shortcut for the nutty depth the toasting creates. Toast fully next time. A knob of butter stirred in at serving helps.
items.cause: Skipped or shortened the toasting step

content.text: Tarhonya turns up on the Hungarian table wherever a braised or sauced meat dish needs something to soak up the sauce. The food writer George Lang calls it a Magyar contribution to the Central European table. The pasta split from the Italian-leaning noodle culture of the old Austro-Hungarian lands and grew its own character. It shows up most often alongside paprikash and pörkölt. It is closely tied to the Hungarian Great Plain, where it was dried in the sun and carried as food by shepherds and herdsmen.
